speaker: LANCE
text: When a man’s servant shall play the cur with

speaker: LANCE
text: him, look you, it goes hard—one that I brought up

speaker: LANCE
text: of a puppy, one that I saved from drowning when

speaker: LANCE
text: three or four of his blind brothers and sisters went

speaker: LANCE
text: to it. I have taught him even as one would say

speaker: LANCE
text: precisely “Thus I would teach a dog.” I was sent to

speaker: LANCE
text: deliver him as a present to Mistress Sylvia from my

speaker: LANCE
text: master; and I came no sooner into the dining

speaker: LANCE
text: chamber but he steps me to her trencher and steals

speaker: LANCE
text: her capon’s leg. O, ’tis a foul thing when a cur

speaker: LANCE
text: cannot keep himself in all companies! I would have,

speaker: LANCE
text: as one should say, one that takes upon him to be a

speaker: LANCE
text: dog indeed; to be, as it were, a dog at all things. If I

speaker: LANCE
text: had not had more wit than he, to take a fault upon

speaker: LANCE
text: me that he did, I think verily he had been hanged

speaker: LANCE
text: for ’t. Sure as I live, he had suffered for ’t. You shall

speaker: LANCE
text: judge. He thrusts me himself into the company of

speaker: LANCE
text: three or four gentlemanlike dogs under the Duke’s

speaker: LANCE
text: table; he had not been there—bless the mark!—a

speaker: LANCE
text: pissing while but all the chamber smelt him. “Out

speaker: LANCE
text: with the dog!” says one. “What cur is that?” says

speaker: LANCE
text: another. “Whip him out!” says the third. “Hang him

speaker: LANCE
text: up!” says the Duke. I, having been acquainted with

speaker: LANCE
text: the smell before, knew it was Crab, and goes me to

speaker: LANCE
text: the fellow that whips the dogs. “Friend,” quoth I,

speaker: LANCE
text: “You mean to whip the dog?” “Ay, marry, do I,”

speaker: LANCE
text: quoth he. “You do him the more wrong,” quoth I.

speaker: LANCE
text: “’Twas I did the thing you wot of.” He makes me no

speaker: LANCE
text: more ado but whips me out of the chamber. How

speaker: LANCE
text: many masters would do this for his servant? Nay,

speaker: LANCE
text: I’ll be sworn I have sat in the stocks for puddings he

speaker: LANCE
text: hath stolen; otherwise he had been executed. I have

speaker: LANCE
text: stood on the pillory for geese he hath killed; otherwise

speaker: LANCE
text:
he had suffered for ’t.
                 
                To
                 
                Crab
                .
               Thou think’st

speaker: LANCE
text: not of this now. Nay, I remember the trick you

speaker: LANCE
text: served me when I took my leave of Madam Sylvia.

speaker: LANCE
text: Did not I bid thee still mark me, and do as I do?

speaker: LANCE
text: When didst thou see me heave up my leg and make

speaker: LANCE
text: water against a gentlewoman’s farthingale? Didst

speaker: LANCE
text: thou ever see me do such a trick?

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Sebastian is thy name? I like thee well

speaker: PROTEUS
text: And will employ thee in some service presently.

speaker: JULIA
text: In what you please. I’ll do what I can.

speaker: PROTEUS
text:
I hope thou wilt.
                 
                To
                 
                Lance
                .
               How now, you whoreson peasant?

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Where have you been these two days loitering?

speaker: LANCE
text: Marry, sir, I carried Mistress Sylvia the dog you

speaker: LANCE
text: bade me.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: And what says she to my little jewel?

speaker: LANCE
text: Marry, she says your dog was a cur, and tells

speaker: LANCE
text: you currish thanks is good enough for such a

speaker: LANCE
text: present.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: But she received my dog?

speaker: LANCE
text: No, indeed, did she not. Here have I brought

speaker: LANCE
text: him back again.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: What, didst thou offer her this from me?

speaker: LANCE
text: Ay, sir. The other squirrel was stolen from me

speaker: LANCE
text: by the hangman’s boys in the market-place, and

speaker: LANCE
text: then I offered her mine own, who is a dog as big as

speaker: LANCE
text: ten of yours, and therefore the gift the greater.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Go, get thee hence, and find my dog again,

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Or ne’er return again into my sight.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Away, I say. Stayest thou to vex me here?

speaker: PROTEUS
text: A slave that still an end turns me to shame.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Sebastian, I have entertainèd thee,

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Partly that I have need of such a youth

speaker: PROTEUS
text: That can with some discretion do my business—

speaker: PROTEUS
text: For ’tis no trusting to yond foolish lout—

speaker: PROTEUS
text: But chiefly for thy face and thy behavior,

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Which, if my augury deceive me not,

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Witness good bringing-up, fortune, and truth.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Therefore, know thou, for this I entertain thee.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Go presently, and take this ring with thee;

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Deliver it to Madam Sylvia.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: She loved me well delivered it to me.

speaker: JULIA
text: It seems you loved not her, to leave her token.

speaker: JULIA
text: She is dead belike?

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Not so; I think she lives.

speaker: JULIA
text: Alas!

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Why dost thou cry “Alas”?

speaker: JULIA
text: I cannot choose but pity her.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Wherefore shouldst thou pity her?

speaker: JULIA
text: Because methinks that she loved you as well

speaker: JULIA
text: As you do love your lady Sylvia.

speaker: JULIA
text: She dreams on him that has forgot her love;

speaker: JULIA
text: You dote on her that cares not for your love.

speaker: JULIA
text: ’Tis pity love should be so contrary,

speaker: JULIA
text: And thinking on it makes me cry “Alas.”

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Well, give her that ring and therewithal

speaker: PROTEUS
text:
This letter.
                 
                He
                 
                gives
                 
                her
                 
                a
                 
                paper
                .
               That’s her chamber. Tell my lady

speaker: PROTEUS
text: I claim the promise for her heavenly picture.

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Your message done, hie home unto my chamber,

speaker: PROTEUS
text: Where thou shalt find me sad and solitary.

speaker: JULIA
text: How many women would do such a message?

speaker: JULIA
text: Alas, poor Proteus, thou hast entertained

speaker: JULIA
text: A fox to be the shepherd of thy lambs.

speaker: JULIA
text: Alas, poor fool, why do I pity him

speaker: JULIA
text: That with his very heart despiseth me?

speaker: JULIA
text: Because he loves her, he despiseth me;

speaker: JULIA
text: Because I love him, I must pity him.

speaker: JULIA
text: This ring I gave him when he parted from me,

speaker: JULIA
text: To bind him to remember my good will;

speaker: JULIA
text: And now am I, unhappy messenger,

speaker: JULIA
text: To plead for that which I would not obtain,

speaker: JULIA
text: To carry that which I would have refused,

speaker: JULIA
text: To praise his faith, which I would have dispraised.

speaker: JULIA
text: I am my master’s true confirmèd love,

speaker: JULIA
text: But cannot be true servant to my master

speaker: JULIA
text: Unless I prove false traitor to myself.

speaker: JULIA
text: Yet will I woo for him, but yet so coldly

speaker: JULIA
text: As—Heaven it knows!—I would not have him speed.

speaker: JULIA
text:
As
                 
                Sebastian
                .
                 
              Gentlewoman, good day. I pray you be my mean

speaker: JULIA
text: To bring me where to speak with Madam Sylvia.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: What would you with her, if that I be she?

speaker: JULIA
text: If you be she, I do entreat your patience

speaker: JULIA
text: To hear me speak the message I am sent on.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: From whom?

speaker: JULIA
text: From my master, Sir Proteus,

speaker: JULIA
text: madam.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: O, he sends you for a picture?

speaker: JULIA
text: Ay, madam.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Ursula, bring my picture there.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Go, give your master this. Tell him from me,

speaker: SYLVIA
text: One Julia, that his changing thoughts forget,

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Would better fit his chamber than this shadow.

speaker: JULIA
text: Madam, please you peruse this letter.

speaker: JULIA
text: Pardon me, madam, I have unadvised

speaker: JULIA
text: Delivered you a paper that I should not.

speaker: JULIA
text: This is the letter to your Ladyship.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: I pray thee let me look on that again.

speaker: JULIA
text: It may not be; good madam, pardon me.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: There, hold.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: I will not look upon your master’s lines;

speaker: SYLVIA
text: I know they are stuffed with protestations

speaker: SYLVIA
text: And full of new-found oaths, which he will break

speaker: SYLVIA
text: As easily as I do tear his paper.

speaker: JULIA
text: Madam, he sends your Ladyship this ring.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: The more shame for him, that he sends it me;

speaker: SYLVIA
text: For I have heard him say a thousand times

speaker: SYLVIA
text: His Julia gave it him at his departure.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Though his false finger have profaned the ring,

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Mine shall not do his Julia so much wrong.

speaker: JULIA
text: She thanks you.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: What sayst thou?

speaker: JULIA
text: I thank you, madam, that you tender her;

speaker: JULIA
text: Poor gentlewoman, my master wrongs her much.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Dost thou know her?

speaker: JULIA
text: Almost as well as I do know myself.

speaker: JULIA
text: To think upon her woes, I do protest

speaker: JULIA
text: That I have wept a hundred several times.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Belike she thinks that Proteus hath forsook her?

speaker: JULIA
text: I think she doth, and that’s her cause of sorrow.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Is she not passing fair?

speaker: JULIA
text: She hath been fairer, madam, than she is;

speaker: JULIA
text: When she did think my master loved her well,

speaker: JULIA
text: She, in my judgment, was as fair as you.

speaker: JULIA
text: But since she did neglect her looking-glass

speaker: JULIA
text: And threw her sun-expelling mask away,

speaker: JULIA
text: The air hath starved the roses in her cheeks

speaker: JULIA
text: And pinched the lily tincture of her face,

speaker: JULIA
text: That now she is become as black as I.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: How tall was she?

speaker: JULIA
text: About my stature; for at Pentecost,

speaker: JULIA
text: When all our pageants of delight were played,

speaker: JULIA
text: Our youth got me to play the woman’s part,

speaker: JULIA
text: And I was trimmed in Madam Julia’s gown,

speaker: JULIA
text: Which served me as fit, by all men’s judgments,

speaker: JULIA
text: As if the garment had been made for me;

speaker: JULIA
text: Therefore I know she is about my height.

speaker: JULIA
text: And at that time I made her weep agood,

speaker: JULIA
text: For I did play a lamentable part;

speaker: JULIA
text: Madam, ’twas Ariadne, passioning

speaker: JULIA
text: For Theseus’ perjury and unjust flight,

speaker: JULIA
text: Which I so lively acted with my tears

speaker: JULIA
text: That my poor mistress, movèd therewithal,

speaker: JULIA
text: Wept bitterly; and would I might be dead

speaker: JULIA
text: If I in thought felt not her very sorrow.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: She is beholding to thee, gentle youth.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Alas, poor lady, desolate and left!

speaker: SYLVIA
text: I weep myself to think upon thy words.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Here, youth, there is my purse.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: I give thee this

speaker: SYLVIA
text: For thy sweet mistress’ sake, because thou lov’st her.

speaker: SYLVIA
text: Farewell.

speaker: JULIA
text: And she shall thank you for ’t if e’er you know her.

speaker: JULIA
text: A virtuous gentlewoman, mild and beautiful.

speaker: JULIA
text: I hope my master’s suit will be but cold,

speaker: JULIA
text: Since she respects my mistress’ love so much.—

speaker: JULIA
text: Alas, how love can trifle with itself!

speaker: JULIA
text: Here is her picture; let me see. I think

speaker: JULIA
text: If I had such a tire, this face of mine

speaker: JULIA
text: Were full as lovely as is this of hers;

speaker: JULIA
text: And yet the painter flattered her a little,

speaker: JULIA
text: Unless I flatter with myself too much.

speaker: JULIA
text: Her hair is auburn; mine is perfect yellow;

speaker: JULIA
text: If that be all the difference in his love,

speaker: JULIA
text: I’ll get me such a colored periwig.

speaker: JULIA
text: Her eyes are gray as glass, and so are mine.

speaker: JULIA
text: Ay, but her forehead’s low, and mine’s as high.

speaker: JULIA
text: What should it be that he respects in her

speaker: JULIA
text: But I can make respective in myself

speaker: JULIA
text: If this fond Love were not a blinded god?

speaker: JULIA
text: Come, shadow, come, and take this shadow up,

speaker: JULIA
text: For ’tis thy rival. O, thou senseless form,

speaker: JULIA
text: Thou shalt be worshipped, kissed, loved, and adored;

speaker: JULIA
text: And were there sense in his idolatry,

speaker: JULIA
text: My substance should be statue in thy stead.

speaker: JULIA
text: I’ll use thee kindly for thy mistress’ sake,

speaker: JULIA
text: That used me so, or else, by Jove I vow,

speaker: JULIA
text: I should have scratched out your unseeing eyes

speaker: JULIA
text: To make my master out of love with thee.
